retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Nephron -- Clinical Significance -- Nephritic Syndrome
retrieved_docs.content: As with nephrotic syndrome, nephritic syndromes can also be primary or secondary. Some of the most common causes of nephritic syndrome are post-infectious glomerulonephritis, IgA nephropathy, and lupus nephritis. Poststreptococcal nephritic syndrome arises 2 to 3 weeks after group A B-hemolytic nephritogenic streptococcal infection of the skin (impetigo) or pharynx. It usually occurs in children but can also occur in adults. No characteristic finding on H&E stain but granular IF pattern and subepithelial immune complex deposition (“humps”) on EM. [24] IgA is the most common nephropathy worldwide. It is characterized by the IgA immune complex deposition in the mesangium of glomeruli. It commonly presents as hematuria following mucosal infections, especially gastroenteritis. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Berger Disease (Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Most patients present with asymptomatic microscopic hematuria or proteinuria that can be present for many years. Hypertension is initially found in a minority of patients but becomes common as the disease progresses to chronic kidney disease or end-stage renal disease. IgA nephropathy can present with gross hematuria, nephrotic syndrome, and chronic kidney disease and can escalate to rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis. Episodic gross hematuria is commonly seen in children and young adults after upper respiratory tract infection. Synpharingitic macroscopic hematuria is a classic initial presentation of IgA nephropathy.

retrieved_docs.title: Poststreptococcal Glomerulonephritis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: PSGN should be suspected in all children with hypertension and heart failure, even in the absence of hematuria or a history of a preceding sore throat or pyoderma.

retrieved_docs.title: Poststreptococcal Glomerulonephritis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: IgA Nephropathy: usually occurs after an upper respiratory tract or gastrointestinal infection, but it differs from PSGN in the shorter latency period it takes to appear after the episode of infection. It can also be described as synpharyngitic hematuria- hematuria and infection coincide.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Nephron -- Clinical Significance -- Primary Nephrotic Syndrome
retrieved_docs.content: Minimal Changes are the most common cause of primary nephrotic syndrome in children. It is idiopathic in most cases, but sometimes it is associated with neoplasias, recent infections, or vaccination. It is characterized by normal glomeruli appearance on H&E stain, negative immunofluorescence (IF) given no immune complex deposits, and effacement of foot processes on electron microscopy (EM). [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Tonsillitis -- Complications -- Systemic Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis This is an immune-mediated renal condition following GABHS infection. Presents with hematuria, edema, hypertension, and low complement levels. Prognosis is generally favorable in children, but outcomes may worsen in older individuals. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Nephrotic Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital syphilis causes congenital nephrotic syndrome commonly in developing countries. Congenital syphilis presents with fever, anemia, persistent rhinitis, hepatitis, hepatosplenomegaly, neurosyphilis, desquamations, and erythematous patches with superficial bullae. Renal involvement varies from microscopic hematuria to nephrotic syndrome. The nephrotic syndrome usually appears between 2 and 3 months. Membranous nephropathy is a common finding in renal biopsy. The immune system is involved in the pathogenesis, as evidenced by immune deposits in the region of the glomerular basement membrane. Prognosis is good as antimicrobial therapy. Usually, penicillin is curative. [3]

retrieved_docs.content: Glomerulonephritis

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Nephrotic Syndrome -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital nephrotic syndrome is more susceptible to infection with encapsulated organisms such as Haemophilus influenzae , Escherichia coli , and Streptococcus due to losses of alternative complement pathway components into the urine. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Severe Combined Immunodeficiency -- History and Physical

retrieved_docs.title: Berger Disease (Archived) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: It is hypothesized that mucosal infection is the culprit of the disease as most patients develop gross hematuria from IgA nephropathy after upper respiratory tract infections, specifically pharyngitis (synpharyngitic). However, most cases of IgA nephropathy are idiopathic. IgA is mainly produced at mucosal surfaces, and its main role is mucosal defense. After the mucosal injury, the IgA1 monomer undergoes a conformational change of its molecule and thus provides the substrate for antigen-antibody formation in susceptible individuals. Galactose-deficient IgA1 is synthesized as a consequence of the mucosal response to various antigens triggered by infections. Circulating immune complexes made from poorly galactosylated IgA1 bind to the mesangial cell plasma membrane, causing proliferation of the mesangial cells with the result of podocyte injury and loss of renal function. [2] [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Poststreptococcal Glomerulonephritis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: PSGN has an excellent prognosis especially in children with complete recovery usually occurring within 6 to 8 weeks. In adults, around 50% of the patients continue to have reduced renal function, hypertension, or persistent proteinuria. [12] [13] Death in adults is often secondary to heart failure and renal dysfunction. Studies show that in the long term some patients may continue to have abnormalities in urine, proteinuria, and hypertension.

retrieved_docs.title: Poststreptococcal Glomerulonephritis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Approximately 50% of children with PSGN are asymptomatic and are discovered accidentally during routine urine analysis. The classic triad of glomerulonephritis includes hematuria, edema, and hypertension. Typically, patients give a history of a recent streptococcal infection such as pharyngitis, tonsillitis, or impetigo. However; some patients develop PSGN without experiencing symptoms of respiratory tract infection or pyoderma, which can be a diagnostic challenge. [9]

retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is closely linked to داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي, an anti‑basement membrane disease of the kidney, and it also forms a key component of متلازمة رئية كلوية, a pulmonary‑renal syndrome that involves simultaneous injury to the lungs and kidneys. This interrelationship highlights the importance of recognizing necrotizing glomerulonephritis not only as a severe renal pathology but also as a manifestation of systemic autoimmune processes that can affect multiple organ systems, guiding comprehensive diagnostic evaluation and coordinated therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1810
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is a severe, necrotizing form of التهاب كبيبات الكلى (glomerulonephritis) that is classified under اعتلال كبيبات الكلى (renal glomerular disease). It is closely associated with داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي (anti‑glomerular basement membrane disease), a condition that can trigger the immune‑mediated injury of the glomerular basement membrane. Clinically, necrotizing glomerulonephritis often presents as متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome), reflecting simultaneous involvement of the lungs and kidneys. Within the broader category of اعتلال كبيبات الكلى, other serious sequelae such as اليوريمية (uremia) are also recognized as child conditions, underscoring the potential for progressive renal dysfunction when glomerular pathology is present. Understanding these interrelationships is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted therapy, and comprehensive management of patients with necrotizing glomerulonephritis and related glomerular disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2508
retrieved_docs.content: Urinary tract disorder (اضطراب السبيل البولي) encompasses a broad range of conditions affecting the urinary system, and within this category lies kidney failure (قصور الكلى). Kidney failure functions as a parent condition for specific renal diseases, including nephritis, nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome. Understanding that nephritis, nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome are subtypes of kidney failure, which itself is a subset of urinary tract disorders, provides clinicians with a hierarchical framework for diagnosing, classifying, and managing these interrelated kidney and urinary system conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2177
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is a severe, necrotizing form of التهاب كبيبات الكلى (glomerulonephritis), representing a specific subtype within the broader category of glomerular inflammation. This disease is closely associated with متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome), a clinical entity in which acute lung involvement—often pulmonary hemorrhage—occurs together with rapid deterioration of renal function. Understanding that necrotizing glomerulonephritis both belongs to the spectrum of glomerulonephritis and can manifest as part of a pulmonary‑renal syndrome is essential for timely diagnosis, comprehensive evaluation of both kidney and lung pathology, and the implementation of appropriate immunosuppressive and supportive therapies to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 917
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is closely linked to anti‑basement membrane disease, a condition that manifests as renal dysfunction, and it is also a key component of pulmonary‑renal syndrome. This form of glomerular injury encompasses the entities “nephritis; nephrosis; renal sclerosis,” which are localized to the glomerulus and represent specific types of proliferative glomerulonephritis. These three entities are further mapped to post‑infectious glomerulonephritis, indicating that they share pathological features with glomerular inflammation that follows an infectious episode. Proliferative glomerulonephritis, the broader category that includes “nephritis; nephrosis; renal sclerosis,” also contains systemic lupus erythematosus (SMQ) as a member, highlighting the autoimmune contribution to this pattern of kidney disease. Together, these relationships illustrate how necrotizing glomerulonephritis, anti‑basement membrane disease, pulmonary‑renal syndrome, post‑infectious glomerulonephritis, and systemic lupus erythematosus are interconnected through shared glomerular pathology and renal functional impairment, underscoring the importance of integrated diagnostic and therapeutic approaches in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1170
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is a specific form of glomerular disease (اعتلال كبيبات الكلى) that is classified as a child condition under the broader categories of nephritis, nephrosis, and renal sclerosis. It is associated with an anti‑basement membrane disease (داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي) and commonly occurs in the setting of pulmonary‑renal syndrome (متلازمة رئوية كلوية). The glomerulus is identified as the primary site of pathology for nephritis, nephrosis, and renal sclerosis, and these entities are mapped to post‑streptococcal glomerular inflammation (التهاب كبيبات الكلى بعد الالتهاب العقدي). Within the hierarchy of glomerular disease, اعتلال كبيبات الكلى also has uremia (اليوريمية) as a downstream manifestation. Together, these relationships illustrate how necrotizing glomerulonephritis fits into the spectrum of glomerular injuries, its immunologic associations, and its clinical sequelae, providing a comprehensive framework for diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2503
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is a specific, severe form of التهاب كبيبات الكلى (glomerulonephritis) and is directly linked to متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome). In the broader context, التهاب كبيبات الكلى is clinically associated with قصور كلوي مزمن (chronic renal failure), indicating that patients with this type of glomerular inflammation are at risk for progressive loss of kidney function. Recognizing the relationship between necrotizing glomerulonephritis, pulmonary‑renal syndrome, and the potential development of chronic renal failure is essential for timely diagnosis, targeted therapy, and comprehensive patient management.

Clinically, necrotizing glomerulonephritis often occurs within متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome), linking severe renal inflammation with pulmonary hemorrhage. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2447
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is classified as a form of اعتلال كبيبات الكلى (glomerular disease) and is frequently encountered as a component of متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome). As a severe, necrotizing inflammation of the renal glomeruli, it represents a specific subtype within the broader category of glomerular pathology. Its association with pulmonary‑renal syndrome highlights the clinical pattern in which simultaneous lung hemorrhage and rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis occur, often due to small‑vessel vasculitis or autoimmune mechanisms. Recognizing necrotizing glomerulonephritis as both a distinct glomerular disease and a manifestation of pulmonary‑renal syndrome is essential for accurate diagnosis, prompt immunosuppressive treatment, and coordinated care of the intertwined renal and pulmonary complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2943
retrieved_docs.content: Nephritis, nephrosis, and renal sclerosis are identified as constituent renal manifestations of متلازمة التفاعل الدوائي المترافق مع كثرة اليوزينيات والأعراض الجهازية (SMQ), the drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms syndrome. These same renal entities are also mapped to التهاب كبيبات الكلى بعد الالتهاب العقدي, a form of post‑infectious glomerulonephritis. This overlap indicates that kidney involvement in the drug‑induced hypersensitivity syndrome may present as nephritis, nephrosis, or renal sclerosis, and that these pathologies share clinical and pathological features with post‑streptococcal glomerular disease. Recognizing this relationship assists clinicians in monitoring renal function, diagnosing renal complications of the drug reaction, and differentiating them from other post‑infectious renal disorders.
